\u4ea7\u6c14\u673a\u7406__\u4ea7\u6c14\u.reason: The item cleanly isolates a single mechanistic judgment axis—how contact-resistance distribution vs bulk resistance determines where heating localizes—so experts must reason about peak local/interface heating without drifting into chemistry or outcome claims. The item cleanly targets boundary-aware safety trade-off reasoning about vent set-pressure in a stiff enclosure, distinguishing pressure-management effects from runaway chemistry and from overconfident claims about toxicity/oxygen/HF. Some options use strong modals (e.g., “always,” “necessarily”) that can make eliminations slightly too cue-driven, limiting discriminative strength from reaching S-tier.

The item cleanly targets a single expert judgment: which added observable best separates early gassing from onset of self-accelerating thermal runaway, with an explicit boundary that gas near ~110°C is not sufficient. It strongly rewards correct regime-discrimination logic (self-heating/internal acceleration) while penalizing common but unsafe proxies (surface temperature/voltage), though the short-answer format can admit multiple acceptable choices (e.g., internal temperature vs pressure RoR) that may slightly reduce decoy-style discrimination.

Overall discrimination is strong, though a few decoys rely on absolute language that can make elimination slightly heuristic.

This short-answer item cleanly targets evidence-bounded engineering judgment: the solver must separate supported cell-level mechanism/abuse-temperature mitigation from unsupported pack-level or “no short” guarantees. The item cleanly targets boundary-aware inference limits between internal trigger definitions (jellyroll/separator melting) and external surface sensing, forcing experts to avoid uniform-temperature and trigger-equivalence assumptions. The prompt tightly constrains the observable inputs (temperature and rise-rate) while requiring an operational three-stage classifier plus explicit non-identifiability limits, which strongly tests boundary-aware engineering judgment (onset vs trigger vs propagation risk) without inviting internal-mechanism overclaims. It is hard to bluff: an expert must separate rate-threshold onset from self-sustaining/exponential trigger behavior and articulate why propagation risk cannot be uniquely diagnosed from single-sensor signals. Minor quality risk remains because as a short-answer item the acceptable threshold values and “quasi-exponential” detection details can vary across competent implementations, slightly widening grading latitude.

\u70ed\u5931\u63a7\u4f20\u64ad\u884c\u4e.reason: The item cleanly targets boundary-aware inference: which cross-placement conclusions are defensible when trigger criteria are multi-cue and when different “severity” proxies can decouple. The item cleanly targets boundary-aware mechanism inference (preheating/energy source) plus mitigation interpretation (propagation stopped while venting can still occur), while penalizing two common overextensions: inferring zero heat transfer and universalizing a module result to any pack. The item cleanly targets conservative interpretation from two partially opposing safety-relevant metrics while explicitly constraining over-causal and over-scope conclusions, which supports strong boundary-aware reasoning. It has high diagnostic value for penalizing the common “higher Ttr = safer” heuristic and for testing whether the respondent can extract an operational hazard-management implication without asserting unobserved mechanisms. Because it is short-answer, scoring reliability depends on rubric strictness for what counts as an acceptable mechanism-consistent explanation versus an overclaim, slightly limiting discriminative sharpness.
